By Anthea Beveridge

Topic:Equestrian

Triple Olympic medallist Shane Rose was thrown from his horse in an accident that resulted in 18 bone breaks.(ABC News: Anthea Moodie)

Triple Olympic medallist Shane Rose has gotten back on the horse, figuratively and literally.

Just over two months ago, the 50-year-old team eventer was thrown from his horse in an accident he has no memory of, which resulted in 18 bone breaks, multiple surgeries and having to learn to walk again.

But right from the moment he fell, he had just one goal in mind.

"My wife played a little clip of when I was on the ground and unconscious, not knowing what I was doing, and my first question was; 'how long have I got to go to the [Olympic] Games?'" Rose said.

"I was always really confident that I could get back, I'm still really confident that I'll be ready to go.

"A lot of things have had to have gone right and, touch wood, so far they've been going really well."

Shane Rose says he didn't feel nervous to get back on his horse Virgil after his fall.(Facebook: Shane Rose Eventing)

Since then, that goal has only become more achievable.

Rose recently returned to his Werombi home, south-west of Sydney, and rode a horse for the first time since his accident.

"I didn't tell anyone how sore it was [getting on a horse for the first time] … but fortunately within a few minutes, [the pain] really eased off and it felt quite good," he said.

"I didn't feel nervous at all about getting back on.

"Every time I've got on it's felt better, from the start right through, and just keeps improving."

Rose spent about a month at the Australian Institute of Sport (AIS), where he lived onsite and had three sessions a day to fast-track his rehabilitation.

Part of Rose's rehabilitation included exercises on a stationary bike with a hobby horse attached to the front.(Instagram: Shane Rose Eventing)

He said the progression he felt in a short time was huge.

"A lot of the work we've done here [at the AIS] has been simulated to try and get me back on the horse and strong in those positions," Rose said.

While the national institution was a far cry from his horse-filled paddocks of home, the staff tried to make his conditioning on a stationary bike slightly more bearable.

"[The AIS staff] got a little hobby horse and strapped it to the front of the bike, so I felt like I was riding a horse even though it was a bit more work than riding mine," Rose laughed.

Ross Smith, head of strength and conditioning at the AIS, says Rose's dedication to getting back in the saddle is admirable.(ABC News: Luke Stephenson)

Head of strength and conditioning at the AIS Ross Smith has worked with Rose since he arrived at the facility.

He said Rose's dedication had been admirable.

"There's still a fair way to go but there's been massive progress on where he's been, so it's been quite amazing how quickly he's recovered," Smith said.

"We're always pushing the limits and going on the horse was pushing the limits.

"We always struggle with going where that limit is and how close we can push to it.

"A horse is a big variable that we don't have control of … when I put him on a horse I have no control, and that concerns me."

Rose, who won silver at the Tokyo 2020 Olympics (pictured), plans to also compete at the Paris 2024 Olympics.(AP Photo: Carolyn Kaster)

Mr Smith's concern was not mirrored by Rose, who had been itching to get back in the saddle to continue to prepare for the Paris 2024 Olympics, which is under two months away.

Fortunately, Rose had confirmed his qualifications for the Olympics as part of the Australian eventing squad before his accident.

But the goal is no longer to just compete, Rose and Smith have now set a gold standard.

"Going to the Olympics and competing at the highest level and striving for that gold medal is where we need to have him at.

"The Olympics is wonderful, but he's at a different level so he needs to be at the Olympic gold medal level, and we'll get him there."

title: Olympic equestrian Shane Rose back on the horse after horrific fall left him with 18 breaks - ABC News
description: Earlier this year, three-time Olympic medallist Shane Rose was bedridden for four weeks after a serious horseriding accident. Now, he's back on his horse with his eyes set on the Paris Olympics.
keywords: act, canberra, local news, local, shane rose, olympian, equestrian, olympic, medallist, local stories, horse, injury, AIS, Australian Institute of Sport, rehabilitation, Ross Smith
